coredata.dc:title: Copper (II)-mediated fluorescence of lanthanide coordination polymers doped with carbon dots for ratiometric detection of hydrogen sulfide
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Jie
Li, Qian
Wang, Caihong
Tan, Hongliang
coredata.dc:description: This work presented a ratiometric sensor for hydrogen sulfide (H2S) based on Cu2+-mediated fluorescence of lanthanide coordination polymers (LnCPs) doped with carbon dots (CDs). The ratiometric sensor (CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb) is fabricated by the self-assembly of terbium ion (Tb3+) and guanosine monophosphate (GMP) on the surface of zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 loaded with CDs (CDs@ZIF-8). In this ratiometric sensor, GMP/Tb moiety was employed as response signal due to the ON-OFF-ON behaviors of Tb3+ fluorescence upon the sequential addition of Cu2+ and H2S, while CDs@ZIF-8 served as reference signal because of the good stability of CDs fluorescence in the presence of Cu2+ or/and H2S. The ratiometric sensor can sensitively detect H2S with a detection limit of 150nM by fluorescent spectrum method and 3μM by naked eyes. Owing to the ultrastrong binding ability of H2S and Cu2+, the ratiometric sensor not only exhibits high selectivity to H2S against other anions, thiols and biological species, which allows detection of H2S in serum sample with satisfied recovery, but also possesses the distinct feature of reversible sensing. We believe that the present study will provide a new insight into the design and application of LnCPs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide coordination polymers
Carbon dots
Ratiometric sensor
Fluorescence
Copper ions
Hydrogen sulfide

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of H2S in serum samples
Detection of H2S in serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb and its working principle for ratiometric detection of H2S.
SEM images of CDs@ZIF-8 (a) and CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb (b). Insets are their corresponding TEM images.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb in the absence and presence of 100μM Cu2+, (b) Emission lifetime of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb alone, in the presence of Cu2+ alone or Cu2+ +H2S, and (c) F545/F450 changes of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb in the presence of Cu2+ with different concentration. Inset is the linear calibration plots of F545/F450 against Cu2+ concentrations.
Emission spectra (a) and XRD pattern (b) of CDs@ZIF-8@Tb/GMP in the presence of Cu2+ and Cu2+ +H2S.

Emission spectra (a) and F545/F450 changes (b) of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb-Cu2+ complex in the presence of H2S with different concentration. Inset (a) is fluorescent images of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb-Cu2+ complex in the presence of H2S under UV lamp. The concentrations of H2S from left to right are 0, 3, 10, 50, 100μM. Inset (b) is the linear calibration plots of F545/F450 against H2S concentrations.
Reversible F545/F450 changes of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb upon cyclic treatment with Cu2+ and H2S.
The detection selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb as a ratiometric sensor in the presence of 100μM various anions.
Determination of H2S in serum samples.

head.ce:title: Copper (II)-mediated fluorescence of lanthanide coordination polymers doped with carbon dots for ratiometric detection of hydrogen sulfide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Jiangxi Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang, 330022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Small Organic Molecule
Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology of Jiangxi Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presented a ratiometric sensor for hydrogen sulfide (H2S) based on Cu2+-mediated fluorescence of lanthanide coordination polymers (LnCPs) doped with carbon dots (CDs). The ratiometric sensor (CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb) is fabricated by the self-assembly of terbium ion (Tb3+) and guanosine monophosphate (GMP) on the surface of zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 loaded with CDs (CDs@ZIF-8). In this ratiometric sensor, GMP/Tb moiety was employed as response signal due to the ON-OFF-ON behaviors of Tb3+ fluorescence upon the sequential addition of Cu2+ and H2S, while CDs@ZIF-8 served as reference signal because of the good stability of CDs fluorescence in the presence of Cu2+ or/and H2S. The ratiometric sensor can sensitively detect H2S with a detection limit of 150nM by fluorescent spectrum method and 3μM by naked eyes. Owing to the ultrastrong binding ability of H2S and Cu2+, the ratiometric sensor not only exhibits high selectivity to H2S against other anions, thiols and biological species, which allows detection of H2S in serum sample with satisfied recovery, but also possesses the distinct feature of reversible sensing. We believe that the present study will provide a new insight into the design and application of LnCPs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen sulfide (H2S) is well-known for its inherent toxicity and important role in various physiological processes. As a highly toxic environmental pollutant, H2S mainly arises from the industrial activities, such as petroleum refining coal gasification and natural gas processing, and is related to the formation of acid rain and many other undesirable environmental and health hazards. In living organisms, H2S is an oxidization product of L-cysteine by cystathionine γ-lyase and cystathionine β-synthase [1], which can act as a signal molecule to regulate various physiological processes, including relaxation of vascular smooth muscles [2], mediation of neurotransmission [3], inhibition of insulin signaling [4], and regulation of inflammation [5].The abnormal level of H2S has been demonstrated to be correlated to many diseases, such as Alzheimer’s disease, Down syndrome and diabetes [6–8]. Therefore, selective and sensitive detection of H2S is of great importance for environmental protection and human health.

To date, many methods have been developed to determine H2S, such as gas chromatography [9], inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission [10], and electrochemistry [11]. However, these conventional methods usually require expensive and sophisticated instruments and/or complicated sample preprocesses, and are time-consuming. Fluorescence, in contrast, has proved to be a more powerful technique for the detection of low concentration targets due to its distinct advantages of high sensitivity, specificity, and low-cost. In particular, the development of ratiometric fluorescent sensors has attracted increasing considerations. Different from traditional single intensity-based fluorescent sensor, ratiometric fluorescent sensor is based on the measurement of relative changes of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths under same excitation wavelength. Thus, the absolute concentration of an analyte is quantitatively determined by the self-calibration curve of the ratiometric sensor, leading to effective elimination of the interference from probe concentration, excitation light source, and background fluorescence [12,13]. In spite of this fact, the current studies on H2S fluorescent sensors mainly focus on the analysis of single-emission signal change, less concerns on ratiometric fluorescent sensing.

Recently, lanthanide coordination polymers (LnCPs) built from the self-assemble of lanthanide ions and organic bridge ligands have aroused tremendous interest because of their unique optical properties, such as large Stokes shift, sharp emission, long lifetimes (up to millisecond), and high photochemical stability [14]. The infinite combination of lanthanide ions and bridge ligands allows to create limitless LnCPs with diverse structures. Compared with molecular lanthanide compounds [15] or inorganic nanocrystals [16–18], LnCPs also show the advantages of superior mechanical property, better processability, and thermal stability [19]. Particularly, LnCPs can eliminate effectively various nonspecific interferences with short fluorescence lifetime via time-delay detection, which cannot be accessed by using traditional organic dyes or nanomaterials as fluorescent probes. Based on these facts, LnCPs have been extensively exploited as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions [20,21], anions [22,23], small organic molecules [24,25], and biological molecules [26–28]. These studies have made great contributions toward the development of LnCPs-based fluorescent sensors, but most of these sensors rely on a single response or intensity varying fluorescent signal, showing poor sensitivity and accuracy.

In this work, we developed a ratiometric sensor for H2S detection based on Cu2+-mediated fluorescence of LnCPs doped with carbon dots (CDs). In the ratiometric sensor, CDs was employed as a reference signal, while LnCPs served as a response signal for H2S detection. The CDs was selected because its broad absorption band, which can extend to the deep UV region to match the excitation wavelength of LnCPs [29], leading to the simultaneous excitation of LnCPs and CDs at one wavelength. Meanwhile, to achieve an enhanced emission and sensing performance [30,31], CDs were embedded into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) to form CDs@ZIF-8. Owing to its excellent biocompatible and selective sensitization effect to terbium ions (Tb3+) [32], guanosine monophosphate (GMP) was used as bridge ligand to construct GMP/Tb CPs. Upon the self-assembly of GMP and Tb3+ on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8, a ratiometric sensor CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb can be formed. The CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb emits two distinguished fluorescence (blue and green color) under one excitation wavelength of 300nm. It was found that the Tb3+ fluorescence of the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb displays a typical ON-OFF-ON behavior upon the sequential addition of Cu2+ and H2S, where Tb3+ fluorescence was firstly quenched by Cu2+ and then recovered by adding H2S, as shown in Scheme 1 . However, the fluorescence of CDs of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb remains constant in the presence of Cu2+ or/and H2S, which allows CDs to be a good reference. Therefore, on the basis of these findings, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb for selective detection of H2S is expected.

All chemicals are obtained from commercial source and used without further purification. Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals are of analytical reagent grade. Terbium nitrate (Tb(NO3)3, 99.99%) was purchased from Shandong Rare Earth Metal Materials Co.,Ltd (Qufu, China). Metal salts, amino acids (Leu, Try, Alu, Asp, Phe, Cys), and glucose were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Guanosine monophosphate (GMP), reduced glutathione (GSH) and N-2-hydroxyethylpiperazine-N′-2-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China); 2-methylimidazole was purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). HEPES buffer (25mM, pH=7.4) was prepared by dissolving 0.595g of HEPES in 100mL of ultrapure water; concentrated HCl and NaOH was used to adjust pH to 7.4. The pH value was calibrated with a pH meter (Sartorius).

The morphology of coordination polymers was examined S-3400 scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Japan) equipped with an energy dispersive spectra (EDS) detector. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, UK) with a xenon lamp as excitation source. For the measurement of emission lifetime of Tb3+, the fluorescent intensities of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb at 545nm were recorded under different delay times and fitted with an exponential function. The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by using Lambda 35 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, UK). The analysis of powder X-Ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum was performed on D8 Advance X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Avatar 360FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) was used to record the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra with the KBr pellet technique.

To obtain CDs@ZIF-8, CDs were first synthesized using a simple and facile hydrothermal method according to previous report [33]. Then, an efficient one-pot strategy was employed to embed CDs into ZIF-8 by the crystallization of ZIF-8 in the presence of CDs. Briefly, citric acid (1.0507g) and ethylenediamine (335μL) were added in ultrapure water with a volume of 10mL. After stirring for 20min to dissolve these chemicals, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel vessel (30mL) and reacted at 300°C for 5h. The final brown-black colored products were subjected to dialysis against ultrapure water to remove unreacted reagents. Next, 1.5mL of purified CDs was mixed with zinc nitrate aqueous solution (125mM, 10mL) and 2-methylimidazole (500mM, 10mL). This mixture was first reacted for 1h at room temperature under stirring and then was kept standing for 24h to obtain CDs@ZIF-8 by the crystallization of ZIF-8 in the presence of CDs. The as-prepared CDs@ZIF-8 were collected and washed with methanol for three times to unreacted materials through centrifugation (13,000rpm, 10min). Finally, the washed CDs@ZIF-8 were dispersed in 4mL of ultrapure water and stored at 4°C in dark.

The formation of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb is based on the self-assembly of CDs@ZIF-8, GMP and Tb3+ in aqueous solution. Typically, 1mL of GMP aqueous solutions (10mM) was added dropwise to the CDs@ZIF-8 solution that were dispersed in 6mL HEPES buffer (25mM, pH=7.4) under stirring. After reacting for 1h at room temperature, 1mL of Tb(NO3)3 aqueous solutions (10mM) was added dropwise. The reaction was carried out for 3h at room temperature. The products were collected and washed by centrifuging. Finally, the precipitate was dispersed in 2mL of ultrapure water and stored 4°C for further use.

The H2S sensing assay was performed based on the Cu2+-mediated fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb. In the fluorescence assay, a cuvette with the length of 1cm was used. An excitation wavelength of 300nm was used to excite CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb, and the emission spectrum was recorded from 400 to 650nm. All the experiments for H2S detection were performed at room temperature. All error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments. Na2S was used as the hydrogen sulfide donor because the pKs of H2S (pK1 =6.96; pK2 =12.90) predict that H2S and H− are the predominant sulfide species in aqueous solution whether H2S, NaHS or Na2S is used [34].

To detect H2S in aqueous solution, the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb suspension (2μL) was first added to 50μL of HEPES buffer (25mM, pH=7.4) and mixed well. Then, 1μL of CuCl2 aqueous solution (10mM) was added to above mixture for quenching the Tb3+ fluorescence. The Cu2+-induced quenching reaction was carried out for 10min at room temperature. After that, different volumes of Na2S aqueous solution in the range of 0–300μM were added to the above solution, HEPES buffer was used to remain the final volume of the mixture to 100μL. The reaction was lasted 5min at room temperature before measuring the emission spectra of these reaction solutions. The emission intensity ratio of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb at 450 and 545nm (F545/F450) was used for quantitative analysis of H2S. For the selectivity experiment, same procedures were used, but H2S was replaced by other anions, thiols or biological species with same final concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2S in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The standard addition method was used to detect H2S level in serum samples. The serum samples were obtained from Jiangxi Normal University Hospital. After the original serum samples were diluted 50 folds for pretreatment, Na2S standard solution were added to prepare spiked serum samples with final concentrations from 0 to 100μM. Then, the procedure same as sensitivity test was applied to detect H2S in these spiked serum samples.

The preparation of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb is based on the self-assembly of GMP and Tb3+ on the surface of CDs@ZIF-8 in HEPES buffer (25mM, pH=7.4). The SEM image (Fig. 1 a) showed that CDs@ZIF-8 is a regular dodecahedron structure with average size of 700±80nm, which is similar to pure ZIF-8 (Fig. S1), indicating that the encapsulation of CDs has no influence on the morphology and structure of ZIF-8. The encapsulation of CDs in ZIF-8 was also confirmed by the FTIR spectra changes of ZIF-8, as shown in Fig. S2. After adding GMP and Tb3+ to CDs@ZIF-8 solution and reacting for 3h, the smooth surface of CDs@ZIF-8 was found to be coated by a polymer film with network structure (Fig. 1b), implying the formation of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb. By measuring the chemical compositions (Fig. S3), it was found that two new element peaks of P and Tb were appeared in the as-prepared products as compared to CDs@ZIF-8. This indicates that the polymer network structure is comprised of GMP and Tb3+. Fig. S4 showed the FTIR spectra changes of GMP in phosphate (from 964 to 985cm−1), CO (from 1683 to 1675cm−1) and C2-N1 (from 1529 to 1532cm−1), suggesting that the formation of GMP/Tb film is a result of the chemical coordination of Tb3+ with phosphate and nucleobase moieties of GMP [35]. These experimental results reveal that the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb has been successfully fabricated.

Benefiting from the extension of CDs absorption band to UV deep region, which allows to match the absorption peak of GMP, the fluorescence of CDs and Tb3+ in CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb can be excited simultaneously under a single wavelength of 300nm. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb displayed five distinct emission bands centered at 450, 485, 545, 584, 623nm, respectively. The 450nm band should be assigned to the encapsulated CDs, whereas the other four bands belong to typical emission of Tb3+, which arises from electronic transitions of Tb3+ from 5D4 to 7Fj (j=3−6). The simultaneous appearance of the typical emission peaks of CDs and Tb3+ also suggests that their fluorescence is independent and does not affect each other. However, in the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of Tb3+ was quenched significantly, while no changes were observed from the CDs fluorescence. Meanwhile, the color of the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb suspension was found to be changed from cyan to blue upon the addition of Cu2+ (Inset of Fig. 2a). Since Cu2+ has no influence on the emission behaviors of CDs@ZIF-8, the quenching of Tb3+ fluorescence might be resulted from the non-radiative transition of H2O molecules as the coordination of Cu2+ with GMP.

To verify this hypothesis, the emission lifetime of Tb3+ in the absence and presence of Cu2+ were measured. As shown in Fig. 2b, in the absence of Cu2+, the emission lifetime of Tb3+ is 1.06ms, which is much longer than that of 316.84μs of Tb3+ in the presence of Cu2+. The longer emission lifetime of Tb3+ reflects that the addition of Cu2+ can destroy the coordination of Tb3+ and GMP, leading to the production of unsaturated Tb3+, and consequently H2O molecules will bind with such Tb3+ and quench its fluorescence via OH vibration [36]. By testing the chemical compositions of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. S5), which was purified by centrifuging, it was found that the Cu2+ was coexisted with Tb3+. So, the H2O molecule-quenched Tb3+ fluorescence is originated from the competitive bind of Cu2+ with GMP rather than replacing Tb3+ from CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb.

Fig. 2c showed that, with the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence of Tb3+ decreased gradually, accompanied by the reducing of fluorescent intensity ratio of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb at 450 and 545nm (F545/F450). By plotting the F545/F450 as a function of Cu2+ concentration, a good linear relationship (R2 =0.9903) between F545/F450 and Cu2+ concentration can be obtained in the range of 8–100μM (Inset of Fig. 2c). On the basis of a signal to noise ratio of 3:1, the detection limit was calculated to be 5.09μM. With respect to the maximum permissive level of 20μM Cu2+ in drinking water set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb as a ratiometric sensor is capable of practical detection of Cu2+ level in drinking water. Nevertheless, when Cu2+ concentration is more than 100μM, the F545/F450 reached to a constant value. This indicates 100μM of Cu2+ is enough to completely quench Tb3+ fluorescence, and therefore such concentration was used in the subsequent experiments to obtain the best sensitivity. Moreover, it was found that the response of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb to Cu2+ is highly specific, almost no changes of F545/F450 were observed in the presence of other metal ions under identical conditions (Fig. S6).

Previous studies have demonstrated that H2S has a high affinity to Cu2+ (Ksp=1.27×10−36), which can react with Cu2+ to produce low-solubility CuS [37]. Thus, the quenched fluorescence by Cu2+ can often be switched on by the addition of H2S. On basis of this fact, many fluorescence sensors have already been developed for selective detection of H2S in the recent years [38–40]. Particularly, such switchable fluorescence behavior induced by the interaction of Cu2+ and H2S has also been achieved on a fluorescent paper sensor [41]. Inspired by these previous reports, after adding H2S, we measured the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb treated with Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 3 a, in the presence of H2S, the Tb3+ fluorescence enhanced obviously while CDs fluorescence still remained constant, which is associated with color changes of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb suspensions under UV lamp from blue to cyan. By contrast, without the existence of Cu2+, the F545/F450 of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb showed no changes in the presence of H2S (Fig. S7). This indicates that H2S itself has no influence on the fluorescence of Tb3+ and CDs. Thus, the H2S-enhanced Tb3+ fluorescence in the presence of Cu2+ could be ascribed to the interaction between H2S and Cu2+. To confirm this speculation, we further tested the emission lifetime and the XRD pattern of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb treated with Cu2+ in the presence of H2S. The results showed that, after adding H2S, the emission lifetime of Tb3+ increased from 316.84 to 672.26μs (Fig. 2 b) and a new diffraction peak at 31° corresponding to CuS can be observed in the solution of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb and Cu2+ (Fig. 3b) [37]. Therefore, the enhancement of Tb3+ fluorescence by adding H2S is a result of the elimination of H2O quenching effect, which arises from the coordination of Tb3+ with GMP again upon the release of Cu2+ from GMP by forming low-solubility and stable CuS.

From the emission spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb, we can see that the emission band of CDs was separated very well from that of Tb3+, and a distinct gap of nearly 100nm between the maximum emission peaks of CDs and Tb3+ can be observed. So, combing with the Cu2+-mediated fluorescence of Tb3+, the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb with dual-emission property would be a potential H2S ratiometric sensor with high resolution. As shown in Fig. S8, the response of the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb treated with Cu2+ (forming CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb-Cu2+ complex as discussed above) to H2S is fast, the F545/F450 reached to a constant value in 2min. Moreover, the solution pH showed serious effects on the F545/F450, the maximum F545/F450 was found at pH of 7.4 (Fig. S9). This may due to be ascribed to the protonation of GMP in strong acid media and the formation of europium hydroxide precipitates in strong base media, which results in the dissociation of GMP/Tb CPs. Accordingly, to obtain best sensitivity, a 2-min reaction time and HEPES buffer with pH 7.4 were used in the subsequent experiments.

Under optimized conditions, the emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb-Cu2+ complex in the presence of H2S with different concentration were recorded to evaluate its detection sensitivity as a ratiometric sensor for H2S. Fig. 4 a showed the fluorescent responses of the ratiometric sensor as H2S was introduced into the reaction solution when excited at 300nm. With the increase of H2S concentration, the Tb3+ fluorescence enhanced gradually, whereas the CDs fluorescence showed negligible changes. The results suggest CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb-Cu2+ complex is appropriate as a ratiometric sensor for the detection of H2S, in which GMP/Tb-Cu2+ moiety function as response signal, while CDs@ZIF-8 moiety served as reference signal for providing built-in correction to avoid environmental effects. Fig. 4b showed the intensity ratio of the emission of Tb3+ and CDs, F545/F450, increased from 0.34 to 0.69 upon the increase of H2S concentration from 0 to 300μM. A good linear relationship (R2 =0.9964) between F545/F450 and H2S concentration was observed in the range of 0.5–100μM. The detection limit was calculated to be 150nM on the basis of a signal to noise ratio of 3:1, which is lower than the level of H2S in healthy human blood (10–100μM) [42]. Compared with previously reported methods for H2S detection (Table S1), the presented sensor exhibited a very comparable detection limit, but it possesses a simpler and faster detection procedure, which does not involve in laborious chemical synthesis and require complicated sample pre-treatment processes or special reaction media. Moreover, the H2S sensing and the concomitant fluorescent changes were clearly visible under a UV lamp, where the blue fluorescence of the sensor suspension turned cyan upon the addition of H2S (Inset of Fig. 4a). In this case, a color change can be identified by unaided eyes, even at a H2S concentration as low as 3μM.

Owing to the ultrahigh affinity of H2S to Cu2+, the reversible sensing property of the presented ratiometric sensor was explored by measuring the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb after alternately adding Cu2+ and H2S. To this end, the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb was firstly treated with Cu2+ for 10min and then H2S was added. When the F545/F450 almost returned to its original levels, another portion of Cu2+ was added. Fig. 5 showed that the changes of F545/F450 is reversible and repeatable upon the alternate addition of Cu2+ and H2S. Even after three repeated cycles, the F545/F450 still remains more than 90% of original value. The results reveal that the CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb can be cyclically used for H2S detection at least three times.

To evaluate the selectivity of the ratiometric sensor for H2S detection, its fluorescent responses to a series of physiological and environmental important anions were examined. The anions employed in our selective experiments included F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, Ac−, C2O4 2−, ClO−, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, S2O3 2−, PO4 3−. As can be seen from Fig. 6 , upon adding H2S to the analysis system, the F545/F450 was significantly increased. However, under identical conditions, almost no changes of F545/F450 were observed in the presence of other anions. Even the concentrations of these anions was presented at 10-folds higher than that of H2S, the F545/F450 still has not an obvious change. The results indicate that the presence of these anions are not able to switch on the fluorescence of Tb3+ quenched by Cu2+, which is attributed to the stronger binding ability of H2S to Cu2+ than other anions. In addition, we also tested effects of some thiols and reactive biological molecules on the fluorescence of the ratiometric sensor, such as glucose, Leu, Tyr, Alu, Asp, Phe, Cys, and GSH. The results displayed that these species induced no obvious change in the F545/F450 either (Fig. S10). Therefore, as a ratiometric sensor, CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb-Cu2+ complex has a high selectivity for H2S identification against other anions, thiols and biological species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2S in serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: On basis of the fact that the presented ratiometric sensor possesses excellent selectivity over biological species, the level of H2S in serum samples was determined by using the sensor to test its practicality. The original serum sample was diluted to 50 folds for pretreatment. The standard addition method was used to detect the level of H2S in serum samples. The results are displayed in Table 1 . It was found that the recoveries of H2S in serum samples are more than 96% and the relative standard deviations (RSD, n=5) are less than 1.58%. The results indicate that the level of H2S in serum samples can be accurately detected with good recovery and precision by using the presented ratiometric fluorescent sensor.

In summary, we developed a ratiometric sensor for H2S fluorescent detection based on Cu2+-mediated fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb. In this ratiometric sensor, GMP/Tb moiety was employed as a response signal, while CDs@ZIF-8 moiety served as reference signal for providing built-in correction to avoid environmental effects. Due to the competitive binding of Cu2+ with GMP, the fluorescence of Tb3+ in this sensor can be quenched significantly, whereas the following addition of H2S can suppress such negative behavior by forming low-solubility and stable CuS to release Cu2+ from GMP. Nevertheless, the sequential addition of Cu2+ and H2S has no influence of CDs fluorescence. As a ratiometric sensor, CDs@ZIF-8@GMP/Tb can sensitively detect H2S with a detection limit of 150nM by fluorescent spectrum method and 3μM by the naked eyes. In addition, owing to the ultrastrong binding ability of H2S and Cu2+, the presented sensor not only exhibits high selectivity to H2S against other anions, thiols and biological species, but also possesses the distinct feature of reversible sensing. Furthermore, the presented sensor has been successfully applied to determine the H2S level in serum sample. We believe that the present study can be useful to promote the development of more sensitive ratiometric sensors based on LnCPs, and therefore finding for wide applications in biosensing, imaging, drug delivery, and so on.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21305054), Specialized Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (No.20133604120002), and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (No. 20151BAB203020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.06.092.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen sulfide: its production, release and functions
H2S as a physiologic vasorelaxant: hypertension in mice with deletion of cystathionine γ-lyase
The possible role of hydrogen sulfide as an endogenous neuromodulator
L-cysteine inhibits insulin release from the pancreatic β-cell: possible involvement of metabolic production of hydrogen sulfide, a novel gasotransmitter
Gastrointestinal safety and anti-inflammatory effects of a hydrogen sulfide-releasing diclofenac derivative in the rat
Hydrogen sulfide slows down progression of experimental Alzheimer’s disease by targeting multiple pathophysiological mechanisms
Endogenous hydrogen sulfide overproduction in Down syndrome
Activation of KATP channels by H2S in rat insulin-secreting cells and the underlying mechanisms
Determination of hydrogen sulfide and methyl mercaptan in mouth air at the parts-per-billion level by gas chromatography
Development of novel and sensitive methods for the determination of sulfide in aqueous samples by hydrogen sulfide generation-inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy
Electrochemical determination of hydrogen sulfide at carbon nanotube modified electrodes
Instant visual detection of trinitrotoluene particulates on various surfaces by ratiometric fluorescence of dual-emission quantum dots hybrid
FITC doped rattle-type silica colloidal particle-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for biosensing and imaging of superoxide anion
Lanthanide luminescence for biomedical analyses and imaging
Recent advances in lanthanide-doped upconversion nanomaterials: synthesis, nanostructures and surface modification
Upconversion nanoparticles: design, nanochemistry, and applications in theranostics
White-light emission from an integrated upconversion nanostructure: toward multicolor displays modulated by laser power
Photoluminescent lanthanide-doped silica nanotubes: sol-gel transcription from functional template
Lanthanide coordination polymer nanoparticles for sensing of mercury(II) by photoinduced electron transfer
A flexible Eu(III)-based metal-organic framework: turn-off luminescent sensor for the detection of Fe(III) and picric acid
A luminescent microporous metal-organic framework for the recognition and sensing of anions
Highly selective luminescent sensing of fluoride and organic small-molecule pollutants based on novel lanthanide metal-organic frameworks
Determination of tetracycline in milk by using nucleotide/lanthanide coordination polymer-based ternary complex
Nanoscaled lanthanide/nucleotide coordination polymer for detection of an anthrax biomarker
Lanthanide coordination polymer nanoparticles as an excellent artificial peroxidase for hydrogen peroxide detection
A terbium(III)-organic framework for highly selective sensing of cytidine triphosphate
Time-resolved probes and oxidase-based biosensors using terbium(III)-guanosine monophosphate-mercury(II) coordination polymer nanoparticles
Analytical and bioanalytical applications of carbon dots
One-pot synthesis of nanoscale carbon dots-embedded metal-organic frameworks at room temperature for enhanced chemical sensing
Encapsulation of strongly fluorescent carbon quantum dots in metal-organic frameworks for enhancing chemical sensing
Energy transfer from nucleic acids to Tb(III) selective emission enhancement by single DNA mismatches
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
A small molecule two-photon probe for hydrogen sulfide in live tissues
Ag+-enhanced fluorescence of lanthanide/nucleotide coordination polymers and Ag+ sensing
Europium tetracycline as a luminescent probe for nucleoside phosphates and its application to the determination of kinase activity
A general chemical conversion route to synthesize various ZnO-based core/shell structures
Development of a highly selective fluorescence probe for hydrogen sulfide
A terbium-based time-resolved luminescent probe for sulfide ions mediated by copper in aqueous solution
Highly selective and sensitive colorimetric probe for hydrogen sulfide by a copper (II) complex of azo-dye based on chemosensing ensemble approach
Fluorescent paper sensor fabricated by carbazole-based probes for dual visual detection of Cu2+ and gaseous H2S
Fluorescent probes for the detection of hydrogen sulfide in biological systems

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jie Gao received her BS degree from Anyang Institute of Technology in 2015. She is currently a graduate student in the college of chemistry and chemical engineering at Jiangxi Normal University.
Qian Li received her BS degree from Jiangxi Normal University in 2013. She is currently a graduate student in the college of chemistry and chemical engineering at Jiangxi Normal University.
Cailhong Wang received her BS degree from Gannan Normal University in 2016. She is currently a graduate student in the college of chemistry and chemical engineering at Jiangxi Normal University.

Hongliang Tan received his Ph.D degree in Biomedical Engineering from Southeast University. He is currently an associate professor at Jiangxi Normal University. His research interests involve the design and development of fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for bioanalysis.
